title: Comb and scroll marbling patterns
subtitle: Using a marbling comb to create nonpareil and bouquet patterns
excerpt: A marbling comb converts a banded base into one of the most refined patterns in Western marbling. The nonpareil (French for 'without equal') is made by passing a fine-toothed comb across a tiger base in a direction perpendicular to the stylus pulls. The bouquet adds a second comb pass in a wavy path to produce the distinctive scroll clusters.
sourceNotes: Joseph Halfer, The Progress of the Marbling Art (translated from the German, first published 1885, public domain via Internet Archive), on the nonpareil, comb, and bouquet pattern families as the central repertoire of classical Western marbling.

glossaryTerms.definition: A classical marbling pattern produced by combing a tiger base in a direction perpendicular to the original stylus pulls. The resulting small, even commas arranged in repeating rows are the defining mark of the nonpareil.

A development of the nonpareil in which a second comb pass is made in a sinuous wave pattern, gathering the combed lines into clusters that resemble flower bouquets. One of the most recognisable patterns in antique bookbinding papers.

The distance between the pins or nails in a marbling comb, which determines the density of the nonpareil. Fine combs at 5 mm spacing produce a small, detailed pattern; coarse combs at 15 mm produce a bold, open pattern.

content.text: begins where the tiger pattern ends. Build the tiger base first, then pass the marbling comb through it perpendicularly. To continue to a
, make a second sinuous comb pass through the nonpareil. The
of the comb determines the scale of the finished pattern.

items.name: Liquid acrylic marbling colours, 3 to 5
items.notes: More colours produce a richer nonpareil. Traditional patterns use three base colours plus one accent.

items.name: Ox-gall, eyedroppers
items.notes: One drop of ox-gall per cup, one dropper per colour.

items.name: Marbling comb
items.notes: 10 mm tooth spacing is a useful starting point for a medium nonpareil. A DIY comb made from a batten with finishing nails works well.

content.text: Drop horizontal bands of colour and pass the stylus from top to bottom and bottom to top as for the tiger pattern. The tiger base should be complete before the comb is introduced: the comb will destroy it if the bands have not been fully set by the stylus.

Hold the comb with its teeth just breaking the surface of the size. Draw it in a single smooth pass from one short end of the tray to the other, perpendicular to the stylus pulls (that is, horizontally if the stylus pulls were vertical). Move at a constant speed. The comb should span the full width of the tray so every row is combed in a single pass. Lifting the comb mid-pass leaves a visible break.

For the bouquet, make a second comb pass through the nonpareil in the same direction as the first, but move the comb in a slow sinuous wave from side to side as you draw it across. The amplitude of the wave and the speed of the lateral movement control the size of the bouquet clusters. A slow wave with a large amplitude makes open bouquets; a fast, tight wave makes compact ones.

items.symptom: The nonpareil looks streaky rather than forming clear comma shapes
items.cause: The tiger base was not built densely enough before combing.
items.fix: Build tighter, narrower horizontal bands. The colours must fully fill the surface before the stylus and comb passes.

items.symptom: The comb drags the size and leaves a wake rather than clean lines
items.cause: The comb teeth are too deep in the size.
items.fix: Reduce the depth of the comb pass. The teeth should barely break the surface film, not penetrate into the body of the size.
